planting.note: Divide established crowns in early spring before growth begins, or plant container-grown plants in moist, rich, humus-heavy soil in part to full shade; udo is a deep-shade forest plant that tolerates very low light levels. It dies completely back to the crown in winter. For edible use, force blanched shoots by covering the emerging crown with a bucket or mounding soil to exclude light — producing tender, white, asparagus-like shoots.

directions: Plant in moist, rich, humus-heavy soil in part to full shade; udo is a large, bold Japanese perennial vegetable native to shaded mountain valleys and forests of Japan, Korea, and China — one of the few perennial vegetables that genuinely thrives in deep shade and also functions as a dramatic ornamental. The emerging spring shoots — harvested when 6–12 inches tall by covering the crown to force blanched growth — are prepared in Japanese cuisine as a crisp, pleasantly bitter vegetable resembling white asparagus in texture, eaten raw in salads, pickled, or lightly cooked. When not harvested for food, the plant develops into an enormous, deeply tropical-looking perennial 6–10 feet tall with compound leaves nearly 3 feet across and clusters of small cream flowers in summer, followed by small black berries. The dramatic architectural scale makes it a remarkable ornamental for shaded woodland gardens. An ancient Japanese vegetable with no close Western analog, combining edible usefulness with the visual impact of a large tropical foliage plant in deep shade.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, fertile to average soil in full sun to part shade; New Zealand flax is extremely adaptable, tolerating coastal salt spray, clay soils, seasonally wet conditions, and moderate drought. Named cultivars with bronze, red, purple, pink-striped, or variegated foliage are widely available and maintain their color best in full sun.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun; New Zealand flax is one of the most architecturally powerful foliage plants for temperate and warm climates, producing bold, erect, sword-like leaves 3–10 feet long in green or in the many named cultivars, spectacular foliage in bronze, dark purple-red, pink-striped, variegated cream-and-green, or orange-flushed combinations. In summer, mature clumps send up dramatically tall flower spikes (up to 15 feet) bearing tubular red-orange flowers rich in nectar that attract hummingbirds and honeybirds in its native range. The Maori people of New Zealand used the leaf fibers extensively for weaving baskets, mats, and garments — one of their most important fiber plants. In warm-climate landscapes (zones 8–11) it provides bold, year-round architectural structure as a specimen, in mixed borders, or in mass plantings, tolerating coastal exposure, salt spray, wind, and drought once established. Smaller species P. cookianum is more shade-tolerant and suitable for partly shaded positions.

planting.note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil in fall; African cornflag is a vigorous naturalizer in zones 8b–10, spreading freely by cormlets to form large colonies on slopes and roadsides. In zone 8 it may need light mulch protection in cold winters. Plant in areas where spread is welcome or where colonies can be naturalized.

directions: Plant corms in fall in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; African cornflag is a vigorous South African corm in the iris family producing upright, sword-like fans of pleated, bright-green leaves and, in late winter and early spring (January–March), strongly arching flowering spikes bearing numerous, bright orange-red to scarlet tubular flowers that are powerfully attractive to hummingbirds and sunbirds. The flowers are curved and tubular — adapted for long-billed bird pollinators — and appear in great abundance on established colonies. It naturalizes freely in zones 8b–11 by producing abundant cormlets, spreading on sunny hillsides and roadsides in California, the Gulf Coast, and other warm-winter climates to form spectacular stands of winter-to-spring color. The variety 'Duckitt's Yellow' produces clear yellow flowers and is more restrained in spread. Outstanding for naturalizing on difficult slopes, sunny banks, and low-maintenance areas where a self-sustaining winter color display is desired.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; dwarf honey bush is smaller and somewhat more frost-tolerant than Melianthus major. Cut back to the ground after frost — it resprouts vigorously from the base. Handling the foliage releases an unpleasant, pungent odor; gloves recommended.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile to average soil in full sun to part shade; dwarf honey bush is the compact species of the South African Melianthus genus — producing the same striking blue-gray, serrated, pinnate foliage as its larger relative M. major but in a more restrained size (2–5 feet) suitable for mixed borders and smaller gardens. The foliage has the distinctive, sharply toothed, almost claw-like leaflet margins of the genus and the same beautiful glaucous blue-gray color that reads as near-exotic in the garden. Dark maroon to brick-red tubular flowers appear on upright spikes in late winter and spring, producing copious nectar that earned the genus its common name — in South Africa, the flowers drip with honey-like nectar attractive to sunbirds and hummingbirds. The bruised foliage has an unpleasant odor (described variously as peanut butter or something less pleasant), so site away from paths or wear gloves when handling. Cuts back to the ground in cold winters in zones 8–9 and resprouts reliably.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in sharply drained, lean, phosphorus-poor, slightly acidic soil in full sun; leucadendron is extremely sensitive to phosphorus toxicity — never use standard fertilizers with phosphate. Use low-phosphorus fertilizer labeled for proteas or no fertilizer in appropriately lean soil. Do not amend the soil with compost or manure.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, lean, phosphorus-poor, acidic to neutral soil in full sun; common sunshine conebush is the most adaptable and widely grown Leucadendron species, a South African fynbos shrub producing lance-shaped leaves that flush brilliant yellow-green to gold in winter and spring — the yellow color intensifying with cooler temperatures and brightening the garden in the season when color is most needed. Male and female plants are distinct (dioecious); female plants produce small, scaled cones surrounded by the colorful bracts; male plants produce more colorful upright flower heads. Many named hybrids including 'Safari Sunset' (dark red), 'Wilson's Wonder,' and 'Cloudbank Ginny' are popular in the cut foliage trade. Like all protea family plants (Proteaceae), leucadendrons require excellent drainage, lean soil with very low phosphorus, and minimal irrigation once established. Outstanding in California Mediterranean climates and dry subtropical gardens; a major component of the international cut-foliage industry.
